root.narration: You're the defensive coordinator calling the shots from the dugout. Top of the fourth, you're up by one with a runner on first and nobody out. Stepping into the box is their cleanup hitter -- a left-handed power bat who pulls EVERYTHING. Your scouting report says 78% of his batted balls go to the right side of the field. He's hit two ground balls to the right side already today. Your pitcher is going to throw him fastballs on the outer half. Time to set your defense.

decision-1.narration: With a dead-pull lefty hitter at the plate, you need to decide your defensive alignment. Your infielders are looking to the dugout for direction. The data is clear: this guy hits it to the right side nearly 4 out of 5 times. But there's a runner on first -- that complicates things. What's your call?

choices.text: Full shift! Move the shortstop to the right side of second base, put three infielders on the right side. The data says he pulls everything -- play the percentages!
Partial shift. Shade everyone a few steps toward the right side, but keep the shortstop on the left. Respect the data but don't leave the left side wide open.
Standard alignment. I don't want to get burned by a shift. If he goes the other way, we're toast. Play it straight.

transition-full-shift.narration: You put on the full shift! Your shortstop jogs over to the RIGHT side of second base. You now have three infielders between first and second base -- the first baseman near the line, the second baseman in the normal shortstop area, and the shortstop in the normal second baseman area. The third baseman slides over to where the shortstop usually plays. The left side of the infield is COMPLETELY open. Your pitcher delivers a fastball on the outer half... the hitter swings!

decision-full-shift-result.narration: The hitter swings and pulls a HARD ground ball toward the right side -- exactly where you predicted! The ball is rocketing between what would normally be the first and second baseman gap. But because of the shift, your second baseman (playing in the shortstop hole) is RIGHT THERE! He fields it cleanly! Now -- the runner from first was going on contact. What's the play?

choices.text: Turn the double play! Flip to the shortstop covering second, then to first! The shift put fielders in perfect position!
Just get the out at first. Don't risk the double play throw -- take the sure out.

outcome-full-shift-dp-great.narration: Your second baseman fires to the shortstop, who is covering second -- FORCE OUT on the runner! The shortstop pivots and throws to first -- DOUBLE PLAY! The shift turned what would have been a single into a rally-killing 4-6-3 double play! Two outs just like that! Your pitcher fist-pumps off the mound. The data told you where the ball was going, you positioned your fielders accordingly, and they made the play. That's analytics meeting execution!

outcome.headline: DOUBLE PLAY! The Shift Worked Perfectly!
outcome.explanation: This is the defensive shift at its best. Let's break down why it worked: 1) The scouting data said this hitter pulls the ball 78% of the time. That's not a guess -- that's a PATTERN. 2) By putting three infielders on the right side, you tripled your coverage of the area where the ball was most likely to go. 3) The ball was hit HARD into what would normally be a gap for a hit. But because your fielder was shifted into that gap, he caught it cleanly. 4) The shift also put your fielders in perfect double-play position because they were already on the right side of the diamond. The defensive shift doesn't change what the hitter does -- it changes where your fielders ARE when the hitter does what he always does. You used the data, trusted the percentages, and it paid off.
outcome.whatToRemember: The defensive shift works because most hitters have patterns. If the data says a hitter pulls the ball 78% of the time, put your fielders where the ball goes 78% of the time. You're trading the 22% chance of getting burned for a 78% chance of making an out.

outcome-full-shift-first-good.narration: Your second baseman fields the grounder cleanly and throws to first for the out! One down. The runner from first advances to second safely. That's fine -- you got the out, and the shift turned a potential hit into a routine groundout. But you left a double play on the table! Your shortstop was right there at second base, ready for the flip. You could have turned two and basically ended the inning.

outcome.headline: Out at First! But You Missed the Double Play.
outcome.explanation: You got the most important thing right: the shift worked! The ground ball that would have been a hit went straight to your shifted fielder. Taking the out at first is the safe play and there's nothing wrong with it. But in this situation, turning the double play was the higher-IQ play because: 1) The runner was going on contact, so the force at second was easy. 2) Your shortstop was already positioned on the right side, giving you a short throw for the relay. 3) A double play with nobody out goes from bases-loaded potential to a near-empty inning. The best defenses don't just make outs -- they make MULTIPLE outs when the opportunity is there.
outcome.whatToRemember: When the shift gives you fielders in perfect position for a double play, take it! The out at first is safe, but two outs are better than one. The whole point of the shift is to create advantageous positioning -- use ALL of it.

transition-partial-shift.narration: Smart compromise! You shade everyone a few steps toward the pull side. The shortstop moves from his normal position to almost directly behind second base. The second baseman moves a couple steps toward first. The third baseman shades toward the shortstop hole. You've got better coverage on the right side without abandoning the left entirely. The pitcher delivers... the hitter swings and pulls a ground ball to the right side!

decision-partial-result.narration: The ground ball is hit sharply to the right side! Your second baseman, who shaded a few steps that direction, dives and snags it! Great play! But the dive took him to the ground -- he can't turn a double play from there. He pops up on one knee. What's the play?

choices.text: Throw to first! Get the sure out -- can't turn two from his knees anyway.
Flip to the shortstop at second for the force! Stop the lead runner!

outcome-partial-first-good.narration: Your second baseman fires from his knee to first -- OUT! Great throw! The runner from first advances to second. One out, runner on second. The partial shift did its job -- that ground ball would have been through the infield for a hit if your fielders were in standard position. Your shade to the right side got your second baseman just close enough to make the diving play. Not as dominant as a full shift double play, but solid defense!

outcome.headline: Out at First! Partial Shift Paid Off.
outcome.explanation: The partial shift worked well here! By shading your fielders a few steps toward the pull side, you turned a hit into an out. That's the core promise of the shift -- turning hits into outs using data and positioning. However, the full shift would have made this play EASIER. Your second baseman had to dive because he was only shaded a few steps, not fully repositioned. In a full shift, he would have been standing right where the ball was hit and could have turned a double play. The partial shift is a compromise: you get SOME of the benefit of the shift while keeping some coverage on the left side. It's a good call when you're not fully confident in the data or when the game situation demands you protect against the opposite-field hit.
outcome.whatToRemember: A partial shift is a solid middle-ground. You get some of the shift's benefits without fully committing. Use it when you want to respect the data but aren't ready to leave one side completely empty.

outcome-partial-force-good.narration: Your second baseman flips from his knees to the shortstop covering second -- FORCE OUT! The lead runner is retired! The shortstop can't complete the double play because the throw from the knees was a little low and he had to scoop it. One out, batter safe at first. You stopped the lead runner from getting into scoring position, which is the smart play with nobody out.

outcome.headline: Force at Second! Lead Runner Erased!
outcome.explanation: Getting the lead runner is smart baseball. Here's why: with nobody out, you want to prevent runners from advancing into scoring position (second and third base). By getting the force at second, you erased the runner who was closest to scoring and replaced him with a slower batter at first. The trade-off is that a full shift might have positioned your fielders for a clean double play instead of a diving play that only got one out. But you still turned a potential hit into an out, which is exactly what the shift is supposed to do. The partial shift gave you enough coverage to make the play without fully committing.
outcome.whatToRemember: When you can't turn two, get the LEAD runner. Erasing the runner closest to scoring is almost always the right call with less than two outs. Don't let runners advance into scoring position if you can help it.

transition-no-shift.narration: You keep the standard alignment. Everybody stays put -- shortstop in the hole, second baseman in his normal spot, third baseman on the line. The pitcher delivers a fastball on the outer half... and the hitter does what he ALWAYS does -- he pulls it! Hard ground ball to the right side!

outcome-no-shift-result.narration: The ground ball sizzles through the gap between first and second base! Your second baseman, playing in his standard position, dives but the ball is two steps to his right -- he can't reach it! The ball rolls into right field for a clean single! The runner from first races to third base on the hit! Now you've got runners on first and third with nobody out! That ground ball was hit to EXACTLY the spot where a shift would have had a fielder standing. You had the data, you had the scouting report, and you ignored it.

outcome.headline: Base Hit! Runners on the Corners! The Shift Would Have Had That.
outcome.explanation: This is exactly why the defensive shift exists. Your scouting report TOLD you this hitter pulls the ball 78% of the time. He did exactly what the data predicted. And your fielders were standing in the 22% zone instead of the 78% zone. That ground ball would have been a routine play for a shifted infielder -- instead, it's a hit with runners on the corners and nobody out. Playing it safe with standard alignment FEELS like the cautious play, but in reality, it's the riskier one! You're putting your fielders where the ball is LEAST likely to go. The shift isn't about being fancy -- it's about putting your fielders in the best position to make plays based on what the hitter actually does, not what he theoretically might do.
outcome.whatToRemember: Ignoring the data isn't 'playing it safe' -- it's putting your fielders in the wrong spot. If a hitter pulls the ball 78% of the time, standard alignment leaves you exposed to the most likely outcome. Trust the numbers.
